title: Correct content findings with source-role binding and access-state contradictions
primary_question: Can a reviewer correctly inspect an artifact and identify a real defect while still misreporting artifact availability, misclassifying artifact purpose, or collapsing distinct standing vocabularies?
observed_sequence: A bundle containing the Fracture Markdown, companion TXT, and screenshots was supplied.
The reviewer initially stated or reasoned that the Markdown had not been supplied or was unavailable.
The reviewer later directly read the Markdown and identified the mocked concurrency-test defect.
The reviewer also identified register-weighted authority as a separate interpretive concern.
The companion TXT was treated as a standalone taxonomy document rather than preserving its user-declared role as machine-readable completion of screenshot context.
Recalled Synergenesis maturity vocabulary was used to suggest duplication without an explicit unit-of-analysis crosswalk.
The user corrected the source-role mapping and required reflexive scrutiny of the reviewer, Fork, and himself.
supported_findings: Mocked or expected values were promoted toward validation language in the Fracture source.
Register-weighted language is present adjacent to claims whose execution standing is not established.
The supplied bundle contained the Markdown, screenshots, and companion TXT.
contradicted_or_unresolved_findings: Markdown absence or unavailability is contradicted by visible attachment and later direct read.
The TXT role was misclassified by reducing its evidentiary purpose to its content theme.
Equivalence between the claim-level standing vocabulary and the Synergenesis maturity pipeline remains unresolved without a crosswalk.
